“Being one of four sisters always felt like being part of something magic.”Blue Sistersis the kind of book that, depending on my mood, I will either detest or adore. Fortunately, this time around, it was the latter. Having given Coco Mellors’ debut a hard pass, I was weary of reading more by her, yet, the premise forBlue Sisterssounded a lot less insipid than the one forC&F. While certainly not flawless,Blue Sistersmakes for a tender, if occasionally too sentimental, exploration of sisterhood, grief, and self-sabotage. It should definitely appeal to fans of the people-fucking-up genre (examples being films like: The Worst Person in the World, Passages, Return to Seoul, Frances Ha and series like The Bisexual) or readers who enjoy complex sibling dynamics (such as inYolk, Sunset, Butter Honey Pig Bread, The Arsonists' City) or female-centered books likeWriters & Lovers,We Play Ourselves, andSelf-Portrait with Boy.“Their family had always been good at hellos and goodbyes, moments ending even as they began. It was easy to love someone in the beginnings and endings; it was all the time in between that was so hard.”The characters are messy and there is a lot of friction among the sisters, so yes, we get a lot of arguments. With the exception of perhaps one or two cases, these come across as very authentic, sometimes overwhelmingly so. Hurtful words are hurled, sometimes with the intention to hurt, sometimes not. Things escalate, but not always. Mellors’ approach to these scenes felt cinematic yet intimate, and I appreciated how she is able to convey the conflicting feelings of her characters. The sisters are often unable to escape the dynamics of their childhood, with Bonnie acting as a pacifier, Avery as the mother, and Lucky as the rebellious youngest one. Avery and Lucky are assholes a lot of the time, something the narrative knows and doesn’t shy away from. Yet that doesn’t make them any less rounded or sympathetic. While Mellors doesn’t use their loss or childhood to excuse their actions, she allows those things to inform our understanding of her characters. I found her very empathetic, and loved many of the reflections around love (be it sisterly or romantic), insecurity, loneliness, and grief.“She was home, the only one she knew, not because she always lived in it, but because it always lived in her.”Through alternating chapters, the novel follows three of the Blue sisters, Avery, Bonnie, and Lucky, a year after the death of the fourth sister, Nicky. At the beginning of the novel, the Blue sisters are in different parts of the globe, but they are all similarly not coping, if not downright freefalling. Bonnie, once a boxer, is now working as a bouncer in LA. She does find herself making her way back to NY, where she is forced to confront her grief, the shame over her last match, and the feelings she’s been long harboring for her former mentor, Pavel. Avery works as a lawyer in London where she is married to Chiti, an older woman who was once her therapist. Chiti wants a child but Avery isn’t ready, in fact, ever since Nicky’s death she has been withdrawing from her marriage. Chiti has noticed but mostly relies on therapyspeak to remind Avery that she too has lost Nicky (as if being reminded of that would help avery…). Avery finds escape in rigorously attending AA meetings. There she meets a younger man, a poet, and their attraction is mutual and has disastrous consequences for Avery’s marriage. Lucky is a model who has spent most of her adulthood in relishing a carefree partier lifestyle. But Nicky’s death has changed things, and now Lucky is not so much as partying because it’s fun, but because as a means of oblivion. After screwing up her latest gig in Paris, she travels to London. Her and Avery’s relationship is more frayed than ever and the two sisters end up driving a further wedge in their bond. Lucky sees Avery as sanctimonious, smothering, and a hypocrite, whereas Avery is exasperated by Lucky’s careless attitude to others and herself. Eventually the three sisters reunite in NY, but their reunion is far from smooth.The prologue serves as a character introduction, one that, through the use of literary devices such as alliteration, succeeded in lending this tale of the Blue sisters the rhythms of a fairy tale. Despite the novel taking place over a fairly contained period of time, the characters have a lot of history with each other and a lot of personal baggage, yet, these forays into the past never weighed down the narrative, and if anything they made the characters more rounded. We come to understand why they act the way they do, the origin of some of their insecurities and anxieties, and why some of them try to escape their grief by avoiding what they once loved, sabotaging their relationships, and opting for self-destructive ‘coping mechanisms’. Bonnie is the more grounded of the sisters, and her arc is not a downward spiral, as it is for Avery or Lucky’s. Still, Bonnie feels responsible for Nicky’s death, and is unsure whether she can box like she used to. Avery has a tendency to shut out other people, something that makes her a hard character to get into. Yet, we can see how hard she has tried to make up for her parents, to look out for her sisters in all the ways they didn’t. She also believes that she was the one to have let down Nicky, but is not fully able to admit this, so she lets her hurt and guilt fester. She misdirects her anger towards Lucky, who is also as lost as she is.I thought that the novel was very self-assured, and that for the most part, it sticks the landing. Sure, one could say that Mellors was trying to cram in too much into the novel. Take the Blue sisters jobs…they are giving ‘try out different careers with Barbie’ (lawyer, model, boxer). They are also too beautiful and not-like-other people at times (Avery and her tattoos…sure, cool aesthetics, but it didn’t seem in line with her character). Even their mother, a character whose presence is mostly relegated to the outskirts of the narrative is subjected to this beautification: “at the time, she had silky auburn hair down to her waist and a beautiful, tulip-shaped face”. While I understood Lucky being beautiful, and her having a troubled relationship with her beauty (she takes it for granted, especially when it comes to what she can get away with, for instance, her beauty glamorizes how unpleasant, rude, and selfish she can be; she is also burdened by it, with other people unwilling to truly see her, or becoming obsessed with her because of her looks, or thinking she is a dumb shallow blonde) when it came to the other characters…these descriptions weakened the novel. They were syrupy and somewhat affected.Avery was the type of lesbian character that feels that has been written by a non lesbian, as in, the writer, in their attempts to avoid clichés about lesbians, ends up writing the straightest lesbian character ever. I did not understand why Avery is made into a lesbian character, given that the person she has an ‘affair’ with is a man…one thing is someone who is still for whatever internal or external reasons unable to identify and/or live as a lesbian, but Avery has been in a relationship with a woman for a long time, she describes herself as a lesbian who is interested in being with women…so why have her cheat with a man? A man she is insanely attracted to. It was a Choice™, one that seemed to me to exist only for dramatic effect (not only she cheats, but she cheats with a man!). Their sex scene also consolidated my perception of her as a very straight character. I just wish the author could have made her bi, queer, or pan. I also find the whole image of the (outwardly) strait-laced lesbian a bit of a bore, but thankfully Mellors does manage to make Avery into a flawed yet complex character. I didn’t like how the cheating plotline is handled,especially when it comes to her being ‘found out’, I found Chiti finding the ‘evidence’ too much of a convenience, and had a hard time believing that Lucky wouldn’t cover for Avery, despite all their issues.Lucky also skates close to being a bit of a cliché, but thankfully the narrative doesn’t romanticize her self-destructive ways. I did found that musician subplot very cheesy—it felt like something straight out of Hollywood—and I thought it was an unnecessary add-on. Similarly, the epilogue, despite the author's heartfelt acknowledgments, felt more corny than touching.However, these aspects didn't significantly detract from my overall enjoyment of the novel. I still loved it (which just goes to show how good mellors can be). The characters and their dynamics were compelling, and I particularly admired Mellors' prose style and ability to establish atmosphere. Mellors also adeptly balanced action and introspection, ensuring that the story never felt either rushed or slow-paced. Additionally, I appreciated that certain elements remained unresolved, such as the sisters’ complex relationship with their mother, adding depth to the narrative. Mellors' portrayal of grief is heartfelt and authentic. Through the lens of the surviving sisters' memories and flashbacks, she paints a vivid picture of Nicky, allowing readers not only to empathise with her but to miss her presence. Mellors' depiction of addiction and the journey to recovery feels genuine and relatable. She captures the struggles and setbacks with honesty, which in addition to making for a candid portrayal of addiction, also made the sisters' experiences all the more compelling and real.I can definitely see myself re-reading this as I found it to be a captivating tale. It had dramatic moments and plenty of emotional beats. Evocative and thoughtful,Blue Sistersmade for a compelling read, full of imperfect people and fraught relationships, all underscored by an undeniable heart. I think readers who are less averse to sentimentality than I am will likely adore it even more than I did. I look forward to Mellors' next novel, hoping that it will align more closely with the style and depth ofBlue SistersthanC&F.I'm grateful for this arc and (depending on my funds) will purchase a copy of my own once it is released.

dysfunctional family, sisterhood, substance abuse, addiction, compulsive disorders, grief and loss, coming to terms with our lives. The writing is quite addictive. The characters stand out and are quite realistic. If you love books by Taylor Jenkins Reid, you will definitely love this one!

Poorly written, full of clichés and overwriting. I’m tired of lesbian characters always needing to have sex with or desire for a man. It’s unnecessary and lesbophobic. The end is predictable, not even wholesome and the last paragraph being the baby’s perspective is just bad.

5 stars!An unforgettable, character-driven story.Sisterhood. A topic I am often drawn to. In this novel, we meet three adult sisters who are grieving the loss of the fourth sister who died a year before. The year since her death has been hard on them, causing a distancing in their relationship instead of a closeness. This story examines them navigating their grief and moving back toward each other.The writing was exquisite! I was completely engrossed and consumed by the beautiful prose. I cared for these sisters immediately. The narratives grabbed at my heartstrings from the start and never let up. Through the complicated and layered sister relationships, the heavy and damaged family dynamic is slowly revealed. Though the girls parents are introduced, their parents are not a central aspect of this story, but the aftermath of their parenting style largely is.This is a slow burn, heavy and deeply moving story. It is one to be savoured and read in larger chunks of time, rather than picked up quickly here and there. The author explores the messiness and all the little intricacies of sisterhood in such an emotional and realistic way. I felt so close to these characters and hoped for a positive outcome for all of them. Each chapter is told from a sister’s perspective, yet the main focus of the book was the relationships they each had with one another, rather than their individual journeys.This was an incredible book that has earned a spot on my 2025 Favourites List. I hope you get a chance to read it!⚠️ There are many trigger warnings, including addiction, overdose and infidelity that I urge you to review before picking this up.

I guess this was the year to read about complicated sibling relationships. I might believe in restoring sibling bonds from 'Intermezzo' by Sally Rooney to Blue Sisters. This was a beautiful and heartbreaking story of sisterhood and grief. When one of the four sisters unexpectedly dies, the family is torn apart, finding ways to grieve--sometimes destructive, other times tender.A sister is not a friend. Who can explain the urge to take a relationship as primal and complex as a sibling and reduce it to something as replaceable, as banal as a friend? Yet this status is used again and again to connote the highest intimacy. True sisterhood is not the same as friendship. You don't choose each other, and there is no furtive period of getting to know each other. You are a part of each other, right from the start. Look at an umbilical cord—tough, sinuous, unlovely, yet essential—and compare it to a friendship bracelet of brightly woven thread. That is the difference between a sister and a friendAvery, the eldest, is a recovering heroin addict—a highly successful lawyer living with her wife Bonnie in London. Avery is feeling stuck in her marriage. Finding it impossible to accept her younger sister's death, she turns to erratic ways of comping. It's more than her sister's death; it's the reality of life; it's deciding whether or not to have a child; it's dealing with the trauma of being the mother to the girls when her mother took a backseat. The girl's father was an alcoholic, forcing their mother to handle him full-time. In response, Avery protected her sisters, taught them life skills, and raised them like her children.Their mother wasn’t really a mother, and Avery covered for her; their father wasn’t really a father, and their mother covered for him. Trying to change them now would be needlessly painful for everyoneMost people go through life never knowing what it’s like to have a calling, one that asks you to sacrifice the pleasure of the moment for the potential of a dream that may not be realized for years, if at all. It sets you apart from others, whether you want it to or not. It can be grueling, lonely, and punishing, but, if it is really your calling, it is not a choiceBonnie, a former boxer, works as a bouncer in Los Angeles. She's been in love with her trainer her entire career but never had the guts to tell him. Her coping mechanism was fighting the next match, training harder, and battling on. Bonnie, in some ways, was closest to Nicky, the sister who passed away—she's left with a level of guilt she shares with no one.The youngest sister, Lucky, modeling since the age of 16, is struggling the most. She parties hard, finds work more demanding since the loss of Nicky, and struggles to put into words the depth of her grief.When their mother informs them that their apartment is being sold, the sisters are forced to come together, rehash broken feelings, and confront what's left of Nicky's life.I don't know much about addiction, but I found it shocking to learn (knowing this is fiction) that three of the four sisters struggled with addiction. It was an addiction that took Nicky's life, an addiction that brought Avery to rock bottom, and Lucky, continuously working in the throes of addiction.When it came to their father:He was the only man in the house, but healso was the house. They lived inside his moodsBut their family wasn’t normal. Addiction whirred through all of them like electricity through a circuitBlue Sisters does a brilliant job of fleshing out each character with enough information to appreciate their journey. Some have likened this to a 2024 version of 'Little Women'--I approve!Not every family comes out of grief stronger, but author Coco Mellors challenges the reader to appreciate the ebb and flow of pain. When resentments and anger are resolved, so much beauty comes from their love and sisterhood. I believed and was devoted to their recovery, whatever way that looked.This was a tender story of sisterhood woven between grief and recovery.4.5/5 stars

2.5 ★this book explores the grief of the three Blue sisters,Avery, Bonnie, and Lucky, as they navigate life after the death of their sister,Nicky. when they learn that their parents plan to sell their childhood home, they reunite, not ready to give up on what they have left of their sister.i want to start by saying this isnota bad book. for the most part, i liked the writing. but i couldn't feel it. because of that, i couldn't connect with the characters. i didn't feel the pain and the grief. i mostly felt annoyed, sad once or twice, but nothing more.: ̗̀➛charactersin most literary fiction books, there's a realness to the characters, as though they could step off the page and exist in the world alongside you. but in this book, the characters felt just a little off. there was something uncanny about them.•Averythe oldest, Avery, has always been the mother figure of her younger sisters. for the most part, i enjoyed reading from her perspective. i found her to be pretty compelling, but some of her decisions frustrated me. i know that's the point, characters should be flawed, but in a character-driven novel, i want to root for them. with Avery, beyond just being mildly intrigued or annoyed, i didn't feel much else.•Bonnieshe was my favorite part of the book. whenever the story shifted to her, i felt excited to keep reading. while again, i had difficulty connecting with the characters, if i had been in Bonnie's POV a bit longer, i'm sure it would've happened with her. second oldest, Bonnie is the shy, soft-spoken one, which is at odds with her former career, a boxer. i thought she was really interesting, and the Pavel storyline made me feel so warm and fuzzy inside.•Nickywe never get to meet her. she exists only in memories, in the spaces her sisters try to fill. if we had a prologue in her POV, just a glimpse of her before she was gone, it could have made a big difference. from what I learned through her sisters, i liked her. i just wish i could've loved her. instead, her death felt distant, and the grief of her sisters never quite reached me.•Luckyyoungest sister, and the one i did not feel any connection to. whenever i got to her chapters, i took a break, i just couldn't bring myself to care. the annoyance mostly came from her parts; i just did not enjoy reading about her at all. i felt proud of heronceand that's the only positive emotion i had towards her.: ̗̀➛writingas i mentioned, it wasn't bad. in fact, there were lines i genuinely loved.“It was easy to love someone in the beginnings and endings; it was all the time in between that was so hard.”but for some reason, the prose didn't resonate with me. i wanted more depth, something to bring out more emotions in me. i felt way too distanced from the story. i found some details the author chose to write to be unnecessary, or maybe that's the result of my detachment from the characters.: ̗̀➛overalli went into this expecting a painful 4-5 star read. sadly, it was not meant to be. the grief of the characters, which was supposed to be the main focus of the book, felt hollow. this book was more about addiction as a coping mechanism, but the way the characters overcame it felt unnatural to me.this is not a book i'll be thinking about. it gets a 2.5—for Bonnie, and for a few quotes that stuck with me.“As long as you are alive, it is never too late to be found.”pre-readi have so many nice, happy books on my tbr, but why would i choose them when i could go for a painful read instead?

RATING BREAKDOWNCharacters: 5⭐️Setting: 4⭐️Plot: 5⭐️Themes: 5⭐️Emotional Impact: 4⭐️Personal Enjoyment: 5⭐️Total Rounded Average: 4.75⭐️Coco Mellors writes some of the most beautiful lines I've read in a novel. Her writing made this book decadent to read, and given the subject matter, I think it's quite a feat. This is a book about addiction and the default roles people take in their families. It's a story about grief, and responsibility, and longing, and failure. By showing a moment in time from three points of view, the reader is given insight into the pain and longing of modern women.I love how delicately Mellors expressed the impact of heartbreak on a woman's desire to forge and grow a family of her own. I love how she explored the ways people manipulate the ones they love most. She deftly wrote the irony of self-sabotage and the magic of having someone believe in you.Best of all, Mellors delivered a redemptive and hopeful end note, that colored the story in shades of healing and optimism. It's just the sort of story worth reading when being human feels like the loneliest thing in the world. It's the kind of thing that reminds us life is complex, and people contain multitudes. I loved this.

book.title: Ricochet
book.author: Krista Ritchie
book.description: As a sex addict, Lily Calloway must do the impossible. Stay celibate for 90 days.Cravings and fantasies become her new routine, but while Loren Hale recovers from his alcohol addiction, Lily wonders if he’ll realize what a monster she really is. After all, her sexual compulsions begin to rule her life the longer she stays faithful to him.Progress. That’s what Lily’s striving for. But by trying to become closer to her family—people who aren’t aware of her addiction—she creates larger obstacles. When she spends time with her youngest sister, she learns more about her than she ever imagined and senses an unsettling connection between Daisy and Ryke Meadows.With Lily and Lo’s dysfunctional relationship teetering and unbalanced, they will need to find a way to reconnect from miles apart. But the inability to “touch” proves to be one of the hardest tests on their road to recovery. Some love brushes the surface. Some love is deeper than skin.Lily and Lo have three months to discover just how deep their love really goes.------Companion to the Sequel: A full-length New Adult Romance that continues whereAddicted to Youleft off. It is highly recommended to be read beforeAddicted for Now.

reviews.text: connor my hubby

“i love you and no other man will ever say those words and mean them the way i do.”“i’m remarrying you, lil. fuck, i’d remarry you a hundred times until it stuck.”“you don’t make me miserable. you make me want to live. and i want to live with you.”“no, lil. you’re the opposite. you’re my stability…my home.”loren hale is so bf. these quotes have seriously done something to me. every time lo calls lily “love”>>>>>5 amazing stars. i’m convinced that none of these books are gonna be rated any lower. idk how, but i liked this book even more than the first one…these books are just going to keep getting better and better, i seriously don’t know if i’m prepared. this book had me blushing, kicking my feet, giggling, smiling till my face hurt, but then it also had me sobbing. again, search up trigger warnings my loves🫶🏼“our i love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain. and every time we say those words, i feel the rush of our childhood. i couldn’t imagine ever losing that.”“he’s my best friend, my whole world.”“he’s always felt like home to me too.”“he’s still lo. he’s still mine.”how can anyone not absolutely adore lilo🥺they are literally so precious. the way they talk about each other and to each other is so🤌🏼i’m in love with the way they love each other. them having to spend time apart while they work on fighting their addictions, but still being so madly in love with each other literally broke me. all lily wanted was lo, but she knew they both needed this, that they both needed to be apart so they can be in a healthy relationship. them realizing that all they were doing was hurting one another and enabling each others addictions literally made me sob because i can’t imagine unintentionally doing that to someone i love :( i literally need to shut up because at this point i’m rambling and sobbing.“how do i bear the hurt of being so lonely without hating myself at the same time?”“hope. that’s all i have to go on right now. it’s my driving force. it’s literally what keeps me standing.”can i just say how proud i am of lily? don’t get me wrong…i’m just as proud of lo as i am of lily but since this book is only in her pov it hits different. you go through her journey of fighting her addiction, with her🥺every time lily cried…i cried with her. honestly i don’t think i would’ve been able to handle going through lo’s journey with him at the same time. they’ve already come such a long way while they were apart. i can’t wait to read the next book and see how different their relationship is compared to how it was in the first book.RAISY AND COBALLOWAY. how is it that this book isn’t even theirs and i’m already obsessed w them!? krista and becca ritchie really knew what they were doing because even tho this book was HEAVY on lilo…i can’t stop thinking about the raisy and coballoway scenes…they’re literally engraved in my mind.i’m a slut for men with the last name meadows, that i know for sure.“then whoever fucked you didn’t do it right, sweetheart.”daisy is stronger than me because i would’ve instantly folded like okay boo show me how it’s done right.GUYS THIS HAD ME DYING…..“did you orgasm at all?”“i’m…i don’t know.”“you didn’t then.”“but i was…”“wet?”“yeah…wait, no, i wasn’t.”“this was your first time?”“no big deal.”“yeah, that’s a big fucking deal. what kind of asshole enters a girl on her first time without getting her aroused first? it probably hurt like hell.”“not really.”“i don’t believe you.” GUYS GUYS GUYS “in fact, you should stay away from any guy who doesn’t make you come at least twice before he fucks you. keep that in mind.”PLSSSSSSS i WILL be remembering this scene when i get to their book. this book was seriously 10/10 and then it was the way connor always shows up for rose when she needs him and the way he GRINS every time he challenges her or literally anytime she’s talking…shut up they’re so cute😭“you’re right. you don’t need a man, rose. but you need me.”yes she does🥹lilo, coballoway, and raisy make up my entire heart and i wouldn’t want it any other way🥺🫶🏼ps: thank you krista and becca ritchie for this amazing series🥹it’s authors like you both and books like yours that keep me going every single day🫶🏼🫶🏼

4.5 stars"Our I love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain."love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love, love,love, love, love, love, love, love, love.🥲"Grab tight,love.""I miss you,love."Talk to you later,love.""Just relax and breath,love.""No yet,love""Hold on,love."“I wish you’re here.” “I am there,love.”"My eyes,love, not my cock," he says, a smile behind the words.You know the moment you’ll close a series it’s gonna hurt when first,you regret not giving a five star to the previous bookwhile reading glimpses of the protagonists’ relationship since their very start and actually thinkinghow it’s gonna be hard to say goodbye to the characterswhen you are only going through the second of ten books or so long series." We started a fake relationship. We ended it. We dated. We loved. And then we separated. Pain, happiness, joy and hurt ricochet from each path taken and from each memory uncovered.One decision can change my life forever."Lily: the novella was more centered on her and how she perceived her addiction,how she acknowledged its repercussions on her relationship with Lo and how in a certain way she kept him trapped in his as well, when she didn’t want to break their "addiction pact" by providing each other the help they needed. Lily’s complex character in the previous book was pretty understandable in how it has never accrued to her to seek for help or to reach for support from her family.But it’s also understandable that for certaines personnes the needto have a contextual response to why she was so selfish or self-centred in "Addicted To You" is necessary, even if it was obvious that her acts and thoughts were dictated by her addiction and the therapist’s sessions highlighted it perfectly."All I’ve ever wanted was for Lo to be happy. I just never thought his happiness would coincide with my depression. It seems stupid and moronic, but in order for him to become healthy, he needs to stop focusing on me so he can worry about his own problems."First, with how Lily had this universal thought and believing thather addiction problem wasn’t tangible because she never had a triggering event to detonate it.It just happened and she just became a sex addict without any traumatizing event. She downplayed what she was going through and believed that everyone would think and do the same."Keeping my addiction from my family has always put this intangible gap between us.""Because I think I know…I think I know that I’ve always felt this way, even before my addiction. Before there were any secrets at all."Second,how she always felt andwas attributed the transparent role between her too charismatic and too successful sisters.How her mother lost any interest in her when she understood that she had "no talent".why do i have the feeling that their mother is equally or more atrocious then Lo’s father and yet she doesn’t receive the hate she needs?The way she treated her daughters in this book was abominable, always running and trying to put together a "perfect family image" to the world whenbehind closed doors she starved one, criticized and slapped another and didn’t even acknowledge the existence of the third, Lily."We have an unhealthy,co-dependent relationship.I know this. "" I want to grow with him.I want to be with him.I want to love Lo without people telling me that our love is too much. "Lo: There weren’t much present-apparitions of his character in the novella butthe flashbacks brought some life into his personage and with their moving and nostalgic retailing, they translated some important events that marked their relationship.I don’t know If I’m an emotional mess right now or if the flashbacks are actually pretty emotional, but I cried a lot while reading them.The way Lo talked to Lily, the way he touched Lily, the way he called her "love" again and again in these segments of their past made his persona so lovable and attachingmuch more than in the first book. When both of them talked for the first time in this book since the last events of the previous oneI cried SO HARD.The way that both of them were finally conscient of the toxic environment that they build together and lived in for yearsand tried/are trying to do better and be better for each other and combat their demons, ugh.. if this is not freaking well done character development.Side Characters:just gonna mention Ryke and how he got on my nervesRyke, his character was disturbing in this book, his devotion for helping Lo in the previous one was admirable but his obstinacy to remind Lily about her addiction and in how Lo was better off without her was irritating. The way he was talking to Daisy wasn’t appropriate, at all, and portrayed his personnage in more of an ugly way.Ps: loved to buddy read this novella with my loves, Aliki and vio, and can’t wait to read the 3rd book altogether and with milo🤭🤭———•———•———•———•———•———•———•——That’s the review 😌Jk rtc———•———•———•———•———•———•———•——Im too CHICKENSHIT to face lilo alone so buddy read with my amazing babesAlikiandvio💜💜Wish us luck..

“You’re right. You don’t need a man, but you do need me.”Okay but that’s literally the hottest statement I’ve ever read.Lily and Loren are both working on their addictions, and I’m so happy that this wasn’t something that is being done quickly. The Ritchies did a great job in making this as realistic as possible.Lily has let a lot more people into her life. I’m glad that almost everyone in her life is really there for her and is helping her in this journey. I especially enjoyed Rose’s involvement. She is a really good sister. It’s important to have these characters to highlight what kind of people one needs in their own life during a difficult time. I’m also glad that we delved deeper into the root of Lily and Lo’s addictions.I also really liked her therapist!! She teaches Lily that she doesn’t need to completely stop having sex, but she needs to find a healthy balance. She also lets Lily know that she shouldn’t feel guilty for being this way. There is usually this guilt for some people having trouble with addictions or their mental health because they feel like they have “everything” they need and other people have way worse problems. This is always a great reminder for everyone.Ryke’s character was really cool. From afar, he seems like a total jerk, but I surprisingly liked it. I just like how real he was. Sometimes we do need those characters who genuinely do not care to impress anyone. He really cares about Lo and Lily and does a great job in helping them get through these tough times.I also like her sisters. They are all different and unique. I saw that Daisy and Rose have their own books, but I don’t know if I’m interested enough to read them.I also really hope to get more of Lily and Lo. Their bond is just everything.I’m hoping to read the next book soon!!! Also, the parents in this book suck!!

4 starsThe minute after I finishedAddicted to YouI started listening toRicochet. Safe to say I am becoming addicted to this series!We started a fake relationship. We ended it. We dated. We loved. And then we separated. Pain, happiness, joy and hurt ricochet from each path taken and from each memory uncovered.This companion novel starts off right after book one leaves off, and it lasts 90 days. 90 days where Lo is in rehab staying sober and 90 days where Lily must live in the real world staying celibate. Both are getting the help they need and fighting their addictions. Even though they’re not together physically, they’re still together fighting their addictions and trying to stay strong for each other.This, like the first book, is entirely from Lily’s pov. I really wanted more Lo in this one, like maybe some of his time away, that’s the only reason it wasn’t quite a 5 for me. I still loved it lots. It was great to get a little more time with Ryke and Daisy- I can tell their book is going to be fun!I listened to this book, and the narration was fantastic. I think the woman who voices Lily does a fantastic job capturing her. Now on to listen to the next one and get my next fix of Lily and (hopefully more) Lo!

── 3.75 stars ୨ৎ꒰ spoiler review ⊹₊ ⋆⊹ ࣪ ˖ "i love you," he says again, "and no other man will ever say those words and mean them the way i do"┆ ⤿ ✉️ ⌗thoughts⟡ this was only the second book & i can already tell i have such a deep connection to these characters. even with lo + lily being so far apart from each other, getting to see them working through their own struggles was so special to read about. i loved how this book dove deeper into their addictions and all the little key factors behind them. as well as lily and lo's relationship in the earlier years of their life. it was definitely needed for the story and helped me to understand everyone more than the first book gave us.⟡ them being able to realize that their relationship was toxic when they were enabling each other's addictions was so important in their healing journeys, and they finally realize thatthey need to get better individually so they can love each other better together.them fighting through their urges to give into the addictions because they love each other genuinely moved me to tears, and i could sob just thinking about them.┆ ⤿ 🎹 ⌗characters⌗ ┆ lily calloway꒰ this is me trying by taylor swift ⊹₊ ⋆lily's healing journey in this book was so important to me. my girl is making slow progress, but she's doing the best she can and i know that she's going to be okay one day. holding herself accountable by telling her sister & her friends is such a brave step and she really is just so strong. i'll be patiently awaiting her future growth in the next books, and while i know her progress won't be linear, i can already tell that one day she's going to radiate light + happiness. as she should!!!⌗ ┆ loren hale꒰ liability by lorde ⊹₊ ⋆my sweet boy, words can't even describe how proud of him i am. him taking the initiate to go to rehab was such a good move on his end, and i know that he's trying his hardest despite everything to get better. he loves lily sm and you can tell by the gentle way he treats her, and once they are both healed ik they will be absolutely stunning together. until then i can only watch their journeys to a safe space while learning to love each other in the process. <3⌗ ┆ rose + connor꒰ stargirl interlude by lana del rey ⊹₊ ⋆when i tell you i have been FEASTING on the little crumbs we are getting of them, i'm not kidding. every little scene with them had me giggling so hard, i will always adore a reverse grumpy sunshine + rivals to lovers book. kiss the sky is going to be one of my favs in the series i'm predicting right now!! rose pushing him away anytime he gets too close is so real of her, she's literally me. and he will always be right there to fall back into her again when she wants it again. they break up, then get back together, then break up again. it's a little toxic right now and i can't wait until they can have their own book and hopefully be smooth sailing from then on.rose was such a good sister in this book, it really shows that she's not just the "ice queen" but instead a compasionate girl with overflowing love for her sisters. her being there for lily any time she needs it no matter how much she may get pushed away is so sweet to see, and knowing that lily will always have at least one shoulder to lean on at all times makes me so happy. connor is this book was so caring to lily as well, because he was her friend first after all. him helping rose get the proper help for lily was so cute, and it's nice to know that there are people in lily's corner outside of her family. :)⌗ ┆ daisy + ryke꒰ invisible string by taylor swift ⊹₊ ⋆awh they rlly prove the invisible string theory right. right now they kind of just exist around each other, always somehow meeting again, and knowing they get together in the future is so exciting to read about. i find it a little... odd sometimes when they were lowkey flirting because, even tho i'm BEYOND excited for their book, she's still 15/16 and he was in his 20's. or maybe i'm just taking it in a flirty way. either way, i loved their banter in this book + seeing how much ryke cares for daisy is so special. when it comes to eating or doing daredevil shit, he's always there to support her and I NEED THEIR BOOK RIGHT NOWW.daisy is actually my fav girl ever in this series so far. she lives life on the edge, but still radiates sunshine everywhere she goes. i can tell there's a deeper story behind her life--she's a model at fifteen for god's sake. and the eating thing had my heart hurting so bad for her because nobody gets her like i do i fear. and ryke, i love him “Each day feels like an obstacle. And a victory.”The missing half star is for lo’sabsenceThe other 4 are for lils journey with her own growth and character development. I wasextremelyproud of her and her willingness to take a chance on herself. Aside from the crumbs we did get of the others and lo especially through lils glimpse into their past and few present day interactions.The closeness and bond between lily and her sisters (esp rose — the sister I’d love to have) and just the intimate overall look we got of everyone interacting here was so enjoyable! I couldn’t get enough. Rose and Connor’s banter was for sure engaging — they’re so alike and I love them sm lol. Idk how I feel about Ryke just yet even though his heart seems like it’s in the right place but certain things that transpired just felt off even tho his support is much appreciated. The support given from mostly everyone was invaluable to say the least.Lily’s journey to recovering from her addiction isn’t an easy one as it isn’t for lo, so I know to expect the highs and lows between them. But for now, I lovedd her ability to push forward to give herself the chance to get better and be open to the help she needed.I admired the inclusion of therapy, in helping her identify where the addiction could’ve stemmed from, and how she can work through that without placing guilt and shame upon herself. It felt like such an important step to getting her on that path that’ll eventually solidify her recovery as well as lo’s. More importantly, so they can be truly happy after much hurt and pain inflicted and enabled among each other“Our I love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain.”As for the few crumbs we did get of lo, they were so pleasing and i enjoyed their few moments. Some of it included diary like entries of lils past memories: from their journey in the beginning as friends, to their relationship as lovers, and lo navigating through his own recovery. Evidently, the connection between them begins to strengthen here (despite his absence) bc of all that they’ve been through and as they grasp what’s crucial to their well-being along the way — they begin to work on improving and abstaining from their toxic behaviors.I loved seeing lo being able to comprehend and doing his best to help lily sort through her own addiction. He wasn’t enabling nor testing her limits especially during that Skype scene (I was internally screaming for lily). As a result of all that and this — the communication between them improved immenselyOverall, this met my expectations, it made me laugh and feel. the emotions were definitely felt here given the writing and this being lily's personal journey with her recovery, and I’m so ready for Lo’s pov finally!Some quotes I loved:“No, Lil,” he tells me with a short laugh. “You’re the opposite. You’re my stability…my home.”“I am transfixed by Loren Hale, my everything.”“I want to grow with him. I want to be with him. I want to love Lo without people telling me that our love is too much. One day, I hope we’ll get there. Hope, that’s all I have to go on right now. It’s my driving force.”“Eyes on me, Lil.” The times he has said this 🤌🏼“I’m some girl who decided to fill the emptiness in her heart with sex. Neglect and loneliness drove me to this place. A single choice to start and then the inability to stop.“Giving something up isn’t the same thing as losing control. It’s the opposite, Lily. You’re taking back control.”“We started a fake relationship. We ended it. We dated. We loved. And then we separated. Pain, happiness, joy and hurt ricochet from each path taken and from each memory uncovered.”

5 stars“I want to grow with him. I want to be with him. I want to love Lo without people telling me that our love is too much.”This book broke my heart more than the first one did. Rereading it made me realise just how much deeper Lily and Loren’s addiction problems run and why it took them so long to disentangle themselves from their destructive and co-dependent relationship. They may not be the reason for their addictions, but they very well know how to enable each other. Still, I am so, so, so proud of them for finally getting the help they need and trying to deal with their addictions in a healthy way. Loren’s father and Lily’s mother played a huge role in their destructive addictions, and it hurts to see how these two rely on each other for all the love and affection that should have been given by their parents in the first place. I hate Jonathan, and I hate Samantha.This book is centred mostly on Lily’s addiction and how and why they are so dependent on each other and their addictions.Ricochetis definitely better than the first one—not just because Lo is in rehab and Lily is getting therapy but also because of the many glimpses and scenes of their past as kids, teenagers, and adults. How Lo and Lily engraved each other into their lives to the point that one can’t live without the other.Lily’sgrowth in this book was slow but so realistic. She did spiral a few times and had breakdowns because she felt alone, but I was glad she was making an effort to get more involved in her sister’s life and with her friends. She’s trying, even though she feels like she doesn’t deserve anything in her life. A few chapters about her past helped me understand her even better—why she has social anxiety but also hates being lonely. She’s been hurt and alone for years, but she just didn’t realise it until she went into therapy. Lily downplaying her sex addiction is one of the hardest things she has to overcome. She always thought that her family or friends wouldn’t understand her fears and urges just because she never had a valid reason or a triggering event that led her down this path. She tried to drown herself in sex because of loneliness, emptiness, and neglect—and her mother is solely responsible for it.“Everyone eventually grows up. But if there’s anything I know for certain in this world—I never want to change without Loren Hale. We have to try to evolve together.”Lorenwas in rehab for his addiction problems. I missed him so much in this book. I really wanted the Ritchie sisters to focus on his addiction while he was in rehab, but sadly, it wasn’t explored much. The flashbacks brought so much attention to his rebellious side and his need to be accepted and loved, which Jonathan exploited. He chose Lo only to criticise him and abandon him. How is it even possible that I am falling for this man on every page of this book? The things he did for Lily and to Lily had me absolutely swooning. The way he called her Love, the things he said to her, the way he reassured her so many times that he would always come back to her, the way he touched her, protected her since they were kids, made excuses to touch her under the pretence of their fake relationship—his I love yous and I miss yous—I love every broken and beautiful piece of Loren Hale.“I love you,” he says again, “and no other man will ever say those words and mean them the way I do.”Lily and Lorenhave been battling their addictions separately for three months now. They have never known how to live without each other, and it was heartbreaking to see them both so sad and in pain. Their fears and what ifs about their changed lives when Lo returns from rehab were so real, raw, and painful. They are both so scared of losing each other—what if the other realises that life is better without them? They have stopped enabling each other, and now they are working towards a healthier, more intimate relationship in which they don’t lie to their friends or family.“Even if I make you miserable?”There’s a long pause before he murmurs, “You don’t make me miserable. You make me want to live. And I want to live with you.”Those few chapters about how Lo saved and protected Lily from the moment they became friends, how he fell first and fell harder for her, and how he would do absolutely anything to keep her at his side—I love them so much. I was a sobbing mess when they had their first conversation in weeks and when Lily felt so relieved that he called her. The first time she saw him on a video call had me sobbing with happy tears. Every interaction, every flashback, every conversation had me crying and smiling. I loved seeing how they have lived through the best and worst parts of their lives together, protecting each other because none of their parents were ready to accept them as they were.“Our I love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain. And every time we say the words, I feel the rush of our childhood. I couldn’t imagine ever losing that.”Coballowaycrumbs were everything! Their lively banter, their stupid 24-hour break-up over mundane things—they are just perfect. I loved the way Connor was there for Rose when her mother slapped her and how he assured her. Rose deserves so much better.“I don’t need you,” she repeats, her voice breaking.“You’re right,” he says softly. “You don’t need a man, Rose.” He pauses and I barely hear him whisper, “But you do need me.”Raisygave me a major ick because Ryke shouldn’t have flirted with Daisy—it was really creepy. Even though their banter was funny and Ryke’s intentions weren’t like that, I still can’t forget that Daisy is just sixteen right now. I love them to bits, but Ryke pissed me off a lot in this book—with his snarky remarks about how Lily shouldn’t feel so sad after Lo went to rehab and how he literally told her she was acting so pathetic. I get that Lo means a lot to him, but he can’t just tell Lily not to feel her emotions! I still feel like, if it weren’t for him, they would never be on a healthy path, but he seriously needs to take a chill pill.➙ Second read: February 2024 — 5 stars➙ First read: August 2022 — 4 stars——————————Rereading bcs i missed my found family 🥺
I loved book one - I liked book 2. I’ll be reading the next one - so many aspects of the characters are relatable so excited to see where book 3 takes us.

4.5 🌟WHEN THEY GET A THERAPIST 😍😍😍I loved this sequel so much!!! This is becoming one of my favorite contemporary series (a bit early to say since I'm only done with two books, but I have a feeling).Objectively, this was so much better than the first book. First reread:i basically cried the whole time 🥲 lily my precious ❤️‍🩹───I loved seeing Lily’s character development and getting insight on her addiction. I loved the scenes with her therapist. I needed more Lo but also understand that it was important to see Lily go through this journey.She loves her sisters but just doesn’t know how to take care of them when she can barely take care of herself. She underestimates herself and what she would and wouldn’t do. She’s the biggest overthinker which is so very relatable, and it’s why I appreciate her inner monologue so much. It feels very realistic, especially with what she’s battling.I love the greyness of all the characters. To be able to see their flaws but still be able to empathize with each character brings them to life. Even the characters you want to hate, that you feel like you should hate like Jonathan Hale, you feel such complex emotions towards him. I hate him one second but then the next he says or does something where I appreciate his character a lot.I loved learning more about Ryke. HOWEVER, I did NOT love the relationship between Daisy and Ryke. I feel very uncomfortable with the way they interact. I felt like Lily was voicing my thoughts whenever she was around them. I’m interested and kinda scared to see how that whole relationship is going to play out though.I think we’re all old enough to feel the scars of our upbringing. Now we just have to find a way to heal.

Only 241 pages but it felt so much longer in the best possible way! I feel like we got so much more out of this than the first book. I enjoyed it way more and I just know the next books will continue to get better as well!!Lily’s personal growth in this book is truly incredible. I loved seeing her battle her addiction. I loved how much support she had with it. I just wish we got to see more of Lo. It would have been so nice to have the POV of his own journey.Our I love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain. And every time we say the words, I feel the rush of our childhood. I couldn’t imagine ever losing that.I struggled to feel Lily and Lo’s relationship in the first book, but oh my I take that back having read this. There are so many throwback scenes in this book that now I truly see how deep their bond is and just how complicated it runs. The first book was all about the toxic side, but this book does a really good job at showing how much they really care for each other. It was so emotional. They still need to communicate beyond their addictions though, but as this is just the start of the journey I’m sure there’ll be some of that in the next books! They’ve already come such a long way and I’m genuinely rooting for them 🥹Again, loved Rose, Connor and Ryke too. Plus, we got to see more of Daisy! These characters gave us some much needed lightheartedness 💜

Our I love yous encompass years of heartache, of hurt, of laughter and pain. And every time we say the words, I feel the rush of our childhood. I couldn’t imagine ever losing that.3.75 🌟 rounded up! Yes, I inhaled it in one day… but God I love them so much it’s not even funny!!! For a shorter book (under 250 pages) this really packed a punch and felt so necessary to not only Lily and Lo’s story but also the rest of the family.I don’t know if I’m unstable or what (I am), but I read this book with a lump in my throat 🥺🥹I am so emotionally attached to these two. I missed Lo so much but I know he was doing what he needed to for himself and for his future with Lily.Lily, this girl… gosh. Her growth in this book made me feel so bittersweet but also so proud of her. Uncovering the layers of her past and the depth of where her struggles began, I just want to hug her so badly.The flashbacks in this killed me, knowing the years of longing between them!!! They truly are embedded so deeply into each other. They don’t exist without one another and now they get to grow into having this dynamic and this deep routed love, but in a healthy way. The way they love each other just feels so raw & vulnerable. As I said in my first review, soul mates.“I love you,” he says again, “and no other man will ever say those words and mean them the way I do.”Listen the yearning between this two is uggh, so good. Loren “you have to earn it” Hale. Jesus Christ this man is going to have me combusting🧎🏻‍♀️‍➡️😩“I’m remarrying you, Lil. Fuck, I’d remarry you a hundred times until it stuck.”“Even if I make you miserable?”“You don’t make me miserable. You make me want to live. And I want to live with you.”I loved getting so much more development between Rose and Connor too!! Also, a moment for the way Connor is with Rose?! Sir. I’m not prepared for you I fear 😧Ryke threw me a few curveballs in this one but I know he is coming from a good place and a place of not knowing what was best for Lo.The sister relationships are everything… I love seeing them slowly open up to eachother and repair their relationships!I understand this was originally a novella but made slightly longer and I think it’s so crucial to Lily’s character growth and healing!!! So for that alone I really enjoyed what this book gave us.Now I get them together again next and I’m so happpyyyyy! (And scared)

can this series last forever? like, can krista & becca ritchie never stop writing about this beautiful storyline? another 5 star read within the series, hands down.lo is away at rehab for the entirety of this book. (yes, he is finally getting help)! therefore this novel is in the point of view of lily and is centered around her getting help as well which i honestly loved. she’s truly such a raw, honest, likable character. and rose is an incredible sister to her. we also get more of daisy in this! the only character i’m not feeling anymore is ryke…shady. but i just savored every sentence of this book. the writing style is immaculate; simple yet powerful. at times my heart ached and other times it smiled, yet i enjoyed every second.the authors did a fantastic job at showcasing the importance of getting help, whether it be therapy or rehab. not only does it change your own life but it trickles down to those around you. and nobody said it’s an easy road but those who truly love you will stick by your side for support. lilo i’m cheering you guys on! i’m so happy to be able to add another lovely 5 star to my collection.*trigger warnings for addiction, mention of sexual assault, and sexually explicit scenes*

“Where are you?” I whisper.“Right here. With you,” he whispers back.I can fully understand now why Lilo is so loved by so many people, myself included now. It's just the way they both are the key to each other's future how they really complete each other. An each of puzzle pieces that really matched. Though in the dark times too but I hope they will be in the bright times soon.Lilo is jsut the characters that you couldn't help but to really cheer and hope nothing but the best for them. Also hoping that everything will work out in the end for both of them. And I do, I really do from the bottom of my heart.Now on to the review.I suppose this is a pretty long novella if I must tell you and honestly there were some parts that I enjoyed and didn't. But mostly I enjoyed each of Lily and Lilo's scenes in this book, I really couldn't careless about the family drama in this book especially about Ryke and Daisy. It just felt like an unnecessary filler for me in the book, in which you'll be perfectly fine if this didn't exist in the book. And since some parts were just annoying as in it was unnecessary for me and I couldn't careless about everything except Lilo, some parts really feel dragging for me. Though I'm not sure why I did feel that in the flashback scene, I was just really leaning towards the present Lilo.About the characters, I really love and I mean love it so much how it turned out for Lily in this book. How she unraveled, how she came into some sort of realization, how vulnerable she was, how in the end she became strong, how she really trying to accept and gave people chance. I love it so much and I think she evolved pretty good in this book, though this is still 2nd book and it's still to early for bigger picture but I still love her. She really turned out great. Loren turned out pretty okay too, I mean I didn't see him a lot in this book and I did feel he was getting better and I love that for him. Can I just simply say that I really love Lilo and how they turned out and really complete each other? I just couldn't wait for more of their story now that he got out from rehab.Now, Coballoway also held a special space in my heart and I just found both of them adorable and so stinking cute. I can't help but wanting to pick up their books immediately. Ryke, on the other hand, turned out to be a fucking jerk and 24/7 pissed and I couldn't for the life of me to figure out why. He was so bad- like so so bad in this book that I really don't like him. His character consistency didn't add up in the first book and I got so many questions about him. Also I'm very uncomfortable in the way Ryke and Daisy interacted in this book considering Daisy was an underage. And Daisy was annoying so was her friends and like I said I couldn't careless about both of them because my solely focus was on Lilo.Now the plot though- frankly it wasn't a mess but it wasn't tidy either. Like it was an abstract and to be honest when I read this I'm not sure where the fuck are we going in this book? But I suppose it served the job, to give us a glimpse how is Lily without Lo while trying to battle her addiction. Was I satisfied with this book though? Well, nope. But I do understand that it meant to be that way so we could read the 3rd book. So yeah- the plot was meh. But since I was just so immersed with Lily and Loren, it didn't bother me that much.I want to give this 4 stars, but I honestly can't considering the plot and how some unnecessary parts were added also with other family drama especially Ryke and Daisy. So 3 stars.I still want to continue this though though. I really hope it gets better.ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡTHANK YOU LILYYA MOM AND ALIKI. I had so much fun with our buddy read! It was fantastic and I really like that we three irrevocably and unavoidably fall in love more with Lilo. Looking forward to our next br with this series plus Milo coming along with us 💖💖I had so much fun and ily guys!!🫶🏻🫂ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡDeciding to go through this with friends 🥲Cause I might have a huge feeling that it'll be pretty hard to go through alone- so yeah.Buddy reading withmy lovely internet mom Lilyyaandmy lovely internet wise bestie AliHope we'll have fun with this read until the end!!😆💖

➳ 4.25 ☆“all i hear is: i’m going to be with you again. i feel like i can breathe again.”people who compare them to magnolia and bj must hate lily and loren because they are not similar at all. i am however, glad i finally decided to continue with this series because i loved this book way more than addicted (book one).➺ lily calloway~ i found her character to be more relatable and congenial as opposed to her character in the beginning. her nodus is not something i can fully relate to, but i am actually so chuffed that krista and becca ritchie decided to give her more depth with this book (even if it's short). typically when it comes to sex—women are not really spoken about this in any conventional light—but i really like her so far, and am rooting for her.➺ loren hale~ nothing much to say about him since he was pretty much absent for the duration of this novella. he is better than i remember him, but i am interested to finally get his POV with this next one. he seems really supportive and sweet though.“i’m remarrying you, lil. fuck, i’d remarry you a hundred times until it stuck.”“and you think i don’t know what my son did to you? i helped him ruin you, you piece of shit,” he says.➺ rose calloway~ i really like her. she is probably my favorite of the sisters. i cannot relate to jackshit when it comes to her brains, but her being the eldest sister who is put off as being cold, bitchy, and discourteous makes me love her more. i just love misunderstood 'mean' girls. while she isn't fully relatable (i don't have that kind of money to know what the fuck is going on), i do think she is the more pertinent character to this type of story telling. in addition, her relationship with her mom is one i am very intrigued to move forward with.➺ (richard) connor cobalt~ not much to say about him because he took a back step in this one. i already know he is my favorite guy as he knows when to shut up, but i like his dynamic with rose too.“you’re right,” he says softly. “you don’t need a man, rose.” he pauses and i barely hear him whisper, “but you do need me.”➺ daisy calloway~ is. she. a. child..... i am confused. trust, i know there are some books (i have read) that are weird, but her entire character in this made me uncomfortable. i just didn't understand why she couldn't have been at least eighteen or nineteen? i am earnest to see what is going to happen with her and her modeling career. especially given her personal struggles and insecurities surrounding it.➺ (jonathan) ryke *what the fuck* meadows~ right so, i am confused. why is he speaking to a CHILD the way he was... i am scared guys. he is my age and i would never look at anyone under twenty the way that he was.... he was extremely brusque to lily for no reason, and the sexual 'jokes' he made towards daisy had me bamboozled. i know they have two books to themselves but i am lowkey scared to even read that because he is really nettlesome. i know he and daisy have thegrumpy x sunshinetrope, but being an insolent bitch at the ripe age of twenty two is not it. especially as a man? sit down hoe.“she’s fifteen,” i say roughly. he shrugs. “maybe she’ll get boobs then.” i. am. TAKEN. ABACK.overall, this series gets compared to boys of tommen and magnolia parks A LOT. while, i can see the similarities and differences, people often tell me this is the best of the three. i am so excited to move forward with their journey. rose and connor are most likely going to be my favorites with the way this is progressing. however, i want a daisy story so bad, but i’m just not sold on ryke right now (?).

જ⁀➴ 𝟑.𝟓𝟎₊˚⊹❤️‍🩹 𝟘𝟜/𝟙𝟜/𝟚𝟝—𝟘𝟜/𝟙𝟞/𝟚𝟝WHAT WAS THAT ENDING?! i thought there was gonna be more 😭 (not in a bad way, though). i think it ends so abruptly because it was originally a novella (1.5) until it was renamed as a normal novel! anyways, immediately moving on to the second book!!lily and lo were ADORABLE in this book!! the yearning, character development, everything was perfect. i usually hate forced separation in books but they did it so well in this one!! lo was only in the book for like 3 scenes(?) but i loved him in each one.this book definitely more focuses on lily’s character development and her growth, which i’m not mad about because i love her. seeing her grow and pass through struggles was really sweet.just based on lily and lo this book would be somewhere around four stars, but i bumped down some points due to ryke…why is this man so annoying?! him constantly talking about lily’s body and then DAISY?! a sixteen year old! every time his name popped up on the page i skimmed…i did LOVE daisy though!honestly if it wasn’t for ryke this book would have been rated A LOT higher but i still really liked it for the most part!! onto the next book!!₊˚⊹ ᰔ 𝐩𝐫𝐞-𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐝:i did say i would try not to rush this series and read them slowly…but here we are 20 minutes after finishing the 1st one!! lily and lo have my heart <3.

3 starsSome choices define us. And in this moment, I make a decision that will change the course of our lives forever.Or maybe, I just prolong the inevitable.Either way, in my heart, I know this feels right.One thing for sure, this series is really addicting, specially because of the wide set of characters. It got me hooked and more interested in reading the other characters’ books. I still not really into Lo and Lily and as much as this made me feel super eager and more excited to get into Rose/Connor’s and Ryke/Daisy’s books. I feel like I need a break from all of this drama. A break from the whole genre, lol! However, I’m still committed to this.Highlights:-The cliff jumping scene!!!-Every Rose/Connor freakin scene. Those two are superior!#1Addicted to you~ 3 stars#2 Ricochet - 3 stars#3Addicted for now~ 3.5 stars#4Thrive~ 3 stars#5Addicted after all~ 3 stars

**** 4,5 stars ****I loved it! I was curious how it will all work out... Lo in rehab, Lily alone for 90 days... and well, it was very well played. Though we didn't have much Lo in present, we had plenty of him through flashbacks from the past. They showed how LiLo's relationship began and why they started enabling each other's addictions.I loved how Lily and Lo realized that they needed to change to have a healthy relationship. Sure, they still have a long way to go but the urge to work on their problems is there.My last tear falls. I can’t move to wipe it. I am transfixed by Loren Hale, my everything.“I love you,” he says again, “and no other man will ever say those words and mean them the way I do.”Beside our main couple and their epic love story, we also got more of Connor and Rose. And you can't do nothing but love this overconfident, know it all, preppy boy and this sophisticated, frosty, independent girl.“She’ll be intelligent and know more than Connor and me put together.”“That’s impossible,” Connor tells her. “We’re the two smartest people in the entire world. You put us together, and you get a superhuman.”Not to mention Ryke... because really... I love him... can't wait for his book. There was so much of him here, it was like prelude to his and Daisy's story.“Hate to break it to you, but sex is ten times better than skydiving.”“No it’s not,” she rebuts.Ryke leans forward on his chair a little. “Then whoever fucked you didn’t do it right, sweetheart.”All in all, loved this one and highly recommend!
